items.q: How is the dependency ratio calculated?
items.correct: (Population aged 0-14 plus population aged 65+) divided by population aged 15-64, multiplied by 100 — comparing dependants to the working-age population.
items.misconception: The dependency ratio is the number of children per family.
items.random_wrong: The dependency ratio is the number of unemployed people in a country.
items.why: It compares life-stage groups, not employment status. High ratios can come from many children (LICs) OR many elderly (HICs) — both create burden but very different policy challenges.

items.q: What is the polar jet stream?
items.correct: A fast-flowing river of air ~10km up at the boundary between the Ferrel and Polar cells, where large temperature contrasts create strong winds steering weather systems.
items.misconception: The jet stream is a low-level wind that pushes clouds along.
items.random_wrong: The jet stream is the air left behind by aircraft engines.
items.why: Jet streams sit in the upper troposphere, not at ground level. They steer mid-latitude depressions; meanders (Rossby waves) explain prolonged heatwaves, cold snaps and flooding.

items.q: What is the difference between physical and economic water scarcity?
items.correct: Physical scarcity is insufficient water supply for demand (e.g. arid regions); economic scarcity is inability to access available water due to lack of infrastructure or investment, despite water being present.
items.misconception: Water scarcity only happens in dry countries.
items.random_wrong: Economic scarcity means water is too expensive for governments to buy.
items.why: Sub-Saharan Africa has economic scarcity — there's water but no pipes, pumps or treatment. Students focus on rainfall; the geographical insight is that scarcity is partly an infrastructure/governance issue.
The answer examiners want is: Twenty-foot Equivalent Unit. “Total Export Unit” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which 2013 Chinese initiative finances global infrastructure?
items.why: It's Belt and Road Initiative. “Marshall Plan” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In which year did Malcolm McLean's first containerised shipment sail?
items.why: Exact answer: 1956. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
